content: The loudness and pitch of sound note respectively depend upon
explanation: Loudness depends upon intensity while pitch depends upon frequency.

content: A uniform metal wire has length ' $L$ ', mass ' $M$ ' and area of cross-section ' A '. It is under tension ' $T$ ' and ' $V$ ' is the speed of transverse wave along the wire. The density of the wire is
explanation:
The speed of a transverse wave is $\mathrm{V}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{m}}}$ where $\mathrm{m}=\frac{\mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{L}}=\frac{\mathrm{AL} \rho}{\mathrm{L}}=\mathrm{A} \rho$

According to the given data,

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & V=\sqrt{\frac{T}{A \rho}} \\ \therefore & V^2=\frac{T}{A \rho} \\ \therefore & \rho=\frac{T}{V^2 A} \end{array} $$

content: A transverse wave is moving along a string. The linear density of a vibrating string is $2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}$. The wave equation is $y=0.02 \sin (2 x+10 t) m$. The tension in the string is
explanation: $$ \begin{array}{ll} & y=0.02 \sin [2 \pi(2 x+10 t)] \\ & \text { Using, } v=\sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}}=\frac{\omega}{k} \\ \Rightarrow & \sqrt{\frac{T}{2 \times 10^{-3}}}=\frac{10}{2} \\ \therefore & T=(5)^2 \times 2 \times 10^{-3}=25 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} \\ \therefore & T=5 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~N} \end{array} $$

content: An open organ pipe and a closed organ pipe have the frequency of their second overtone identical. The ratio of length of closed pipe to that of open pipe is (Neglect end correction)
explanation:
Second overtone of pipe closed at one end is

$$ \mathrm{n}=\frac{5 \mathrm{v}}{4 l} $$

Second overtone of pipe open at both the end is

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \mathrm{n}^{\prime}=3\left(\frac{\mathrm{v}}{2 l^{\prime}}\right) \\ & \mathrm{n}^{\prime}=\mathrm{n}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, \text { ...(given) }\\ \therefore & \frac{3 \mathrm{v}}{2 l^{\prime}}=\frac{5 \mathrm{v}}{4 l} \\ \therefore & \frac{l}{l^{\prime}}=\frac{5}{6} . \end{array} $$

content: A wave is travelling in the negative x direction having displacement 3 cm along Y direction, wavelength $2 \pi \mathrm{~m}$ and frequency $\left(\frac{1}{2 \pi}\right) \mathrm{Hz}$ is represented as ( $t=$ time)
explanation:
$$ \mathrm{A}=3 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}, \lambda=2 \pi \mathrm{~m}, \mathrm{n}=\left(\frac{1}{2 \pi}\right) \mathrm{Hz} $$

General equation of wave travelling in negative x-direction,

$$ \begin{aligned} & y=A \sin \left(\omega t+\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} x\right) \\ \therefore \quad & y=3 \times 10^{-2} \sin \left(2 \pi\left(\frac{1}{2 \pi}\right) t+\frac{2 \pi}{2 \pi} x\right) \ldots[\because \omega=2 \pi n] \\ \therefore \quad & y=3 \times 10^{-2} \sin (t+x) m \end{aligned} $$

content: A passenger is sitting in a train which is moving fast. The engine blows a whistle of frequency ' n '. If the apparent frequency of sound heard by the passenger is $n^1$ then
explanation:
Given:

  • The train (including the engine and the passenger) is moving fast.

  • The engine blows a whistle of frequency $ n $.

  • The passenger is sitting in the train (so, moves along with the engine).

The apparent frequency of the sound heard by the passenger is $ n^1 $.

We need to find the correct relation between $ n^1 $ and $ n $.

Concept:

The apparent frequency heard by an observer depends on the relative motion between the source and the observer.

$ n' = n \left( \frac{v + v_o}{v - v_s} \right) $

where

  • $ v $: speed of sound

  • $ v_o $: velocity of observer towards source (positive if moving toward)

  • $ v_s $: velocity of source away from observer (positive if moving away)

Here:

Both the source (engine) and observer (passenger) are in the same train, moving with the same velocity.

→ There is no relative motion between source and observer.

Hence:

$ n^1 = n $

✅ Correct Option: D

$ \boxed{n^1 = n} $

content: Two points on a travelling wave having frequency 500 Hz and velocity $300 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ are $30^{\circ}$ out of phase, then the minimum distance between the two points is
explanation:
Given data

  • Frequency, $ f = 500\ \text{Hz} $

  • Wave velocity, $ v = 300\ \text{m/s} $

  • Phase difference, $ \Delta\phi = 30^\circ = \dfrac{\pi}{6}\ \text{radians} $

We need the minimum distance between two points that have this phase difference.

Step 1: Relation between phase difference and path difference

$ \frac{\Delta\phi}{2\pi} = \frac{\Delta x}{\lambda} $

Hence,

$ \Delta x = \frac{\Delta\phi}{2\pi}\,\lambda $

Step 2: Wavelength

$ \lambda = \frac{v}{f} = \frac{300}{500} = 0.6\ \text{m} $

Step 3: Substitute values

$ \Delta x = \frac{(\pi/6)}{2\pi} \times 0.6 = \frac{1}{12} \times 0.6 = 0.05\ \text{m} $

✅ Answer:

$ \boxed{0.05\ \text{m}} $

Correct Option: (C) 0.05 m

content: A simple harmonic progressive wave is given by $y=A \sin 2 \pi\left(n t-\frac{x}{\lambda}\right)$. If the wave velocity is equal to $=\frac{1}{3}$ (maximum particle velocity) then the wavelength $\lambda$ is given by
explanation:
Given wave equation

$ y = A \sin 2\pi \left( n t - \frac{x}{\lambda} \right) $

Compare with the standard wave form:

$ y = A \sin ( \omega t - kx ) $

So,

$ \omega = 2\pi n \quad \text{and} \quad k = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} $

Step 1: Particle velocity

Particle velocity = time derivative of $ y $:

$ v_p = \frac{\partial y}{\partial t} = A (2\pi n) \cos 2\pi\left(nt - \frac{x}{\lambda}\right) $

Maximum particle velocity:

$ |v_p|_{\max} = 2\pi n A $

Step 2: Wave velocity

Wave velocity (phase velocity):

$ v = \frac{\omega}{k} = \frac{2\pi n}{2\pi/\lambda} = n \lambda $

Step 3: Given condition

$ v = \frac{1}{3} (\text{maximum particle velocity}) $

That is,

$ n \lambda = \frac{1}{3} (2\pi n A) $

Step 4: Solve for λ

Cancel $n$ (since $n \neq 0$):

$ \lambda = \frac{2\pi A}{3} $

✅ Final Answer:

$ \boxed{\lambda = \frac{2\pi A}{3}} $

Correct Option: A

content: The third overtone of a closed pipe of length ' $\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{c}}$ ' has the same frequency as the third overtone of an open pipe of length ' $L_0$ '. The ratio ' $\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{c}}$ ': ' $\mathrm{L}_0$ ' is equal to (Neglecting end correction)
explanation:
Step 1: Write the formula for the frequency of overtones

  • For a closed pipe, the frequency of the $n^\text{th}$ overtone is:

    $$ f_{n,\ \text{closed}} = (2n + 1) \dfrac{v}{4L_c} $$

    where $n=0$ is the fundamental, $n=1$ the first overtone, and so on.

  • For an open pipe, the frequency of the $n^\text{th}$ overtone is:

    $$ f_{n,\ \text{open}} = (n+1)\dfrac{v}{2L_0} $$

    where $n=0$ is the fundamental, $n=1$ the first overtone, etc.

Step 2: Identify which overtone is asked

  • Third overtone ($n=3$) for both pipes.

  • For closed pipe: third overtone means $n=3$.

  • For open pipe: third overtone means $n=3$.

Step 3: Write the frequency for each

Closed pipe ($n=3$):

$$ f_{3,\ \text{closed}} = (2 \times 3 + 1) \dfrac{v}{4L_c} = 7 \dfrac{v}{4L_c} $$

Open pipe ($n=3$):

$$ f_{3,\ \text{open}} = (3 + 1) \dfrac{v}{2L_0} = 4 \dfrac{v}{2L_0} = 2 \dfrac{v}{L_0} $$

Step 4: Set the frequencies equal and find the ratio

Given:

$$ f_{3,\ \text{closed}} = f_{3,\ \text{open}} $$

Therefore:

$$ \frac{7v}{4L_c} = \frac{2v}{L_0} $$

Cancel $v$ from both sides:

$$ \frac{7}{4L_c} = \frac{2}{L_0} $$

Cross-multiplied:

$$ 7L_0 = 8L_c $$

Step 5: Find the required ratio

$$ \frac{L_c}{L_0} = \frac{7}{8} $$

So, $L_c : L_0 = 7 : 8$

Final Answer:

Option D: $7 : 8$

content: Two sounding sources send waves at certain temperature in air of wavelength 60 cm and 60.6 cm respectively. The frequency of sources differ by 5 Hz . The velocity of sound in air at same temperature is
explanation:
Given:

  • Wavelengths: $\lambda_1 = 60~\text{cm}$, $\lambda_2 = 60.6~\text{cm}$

  • Difference in frequency: $|f_1 - f_2| = 5~\text{Hz}$

Let the velocity of sound be $v$

Step 1: Write relation between frequency, wavelength, and velocity

For a wave,

$$ v = f \lambda $$

So,

$$ f_1 = \frac{v}{\lambda_1} $$

$$ f_2 = \frac{v}{\lambda_2} $$

Step 2: Difference in frequency

Given:

$$ |f_1 - f_2| = 5 $$

Substituting the values:

$$ \left|\frac{v}{\lambda_1} - \frac{v}{\lambda_2}\right| = 5 $$

$$ v \left|\frac{1}{\lambda_1} - \frac{1}{\lambda_2}\right| = 5 $$

Step 3: Simplify the wavelength terms

$\lambda_1 = 60$ cm $= 0.60$ m, $\lambda_2 = 60.6$ cm $= 0.606$ m

$$ \frac{1}{0.60} - \frac{1}{0.606} = \frac{0.606 - 0.60}{0.60 \times 0.606} $$

$$ = \frac{0.006}{0.3636} $$

$$ \approx 0.0165 $$

Step 4: Substitute back and solve for $v$

$$ v \times 0.0165 = 5 $$

$$ v = \frac{5}{0.0165} $$

$$ v \approx 303~\text{m/s} $$

Final Answer:

Option C

$$ \boxed{303~\text{m/s}} $$

content: A simple harmonic progressive wave is given by equation $y=\operatorname{asin} 2 \pi\left(n t-\frac{x}{\lambda}\right)$. If the wave velocity is equal to $\frac{1}{4} \times$ (maximum particle velocity), then the wavelength ' $\lambda$ ' is (Given $\rightarrow \mathrm{a}=$ amplitude, $\mathrm{n}=$ frequency, $\mathrm{t}=$ time, $\mathrm{y}=$ displacement, $\mathrm{x}=$ distance )
explanation:
Given wave equation:

$ y = a \sin 2\pi \left(n t - \frac{x}{\lambda}\right) $

Let's find:

  • Wave velocity $(v)$

  • Maximum particle velocity $(v_{\text{max}})$

  • Relate them as per the question to get $\lambda$.

1. Wave velocity $(v)$

For a progressive wave:

$ v = n \lambda $

where $n$ is frequency, $\lambda$ is wavelength.

2. Maximum particle velocity $(v_{\text{max}})$

Maximum particle velocity is:

$ v_{\text{max}} = \omega a $

Here, angular frequency $\omega = 2 \pi n$, so

$ v_{\text{max}} = (2 \pi n) a $

3. Given Condition

Wave velocity is $\dfrac{1}{4}$ times maximum particle velocity:

$ v = \frac{1}{4} v_{\text{max}} $

Substitute the expressions:

$ n \lambda = \frac{1}{4} (2\pi n a) $

Divide both sides by $n$ (assuming $n \neq 0$):

$ \lambda = \frac{1}{4} \cdot 2 \pi a $

$ \lambda = \frac{2 \pi a}{4} $

$ \lambda = \frac{\pi a}{2} $

4. Final Answer

$ \boxed{\frac{\pi a}{2}} $

Option A is correct.

content: Three tuning forks $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$ and C have respective frequencies $\mathrm{n}_1, \mathrm{n}_2$ and $\mathrm{n}_3$ related as $\mathrm{n}_1=1.03 \mathrm{n}_2$ and $n_3=0.99 n_2$. When $A$ and $C$ are sounded together 4 beats are heard per second. The frequencies of fork B and C are respectively
explanation:
Given:

  • $n_1 = 1.03 n_2$

  • $n_3 = 0.99 n_2$

  • When A and C are sounded together, 4 beats per second are heard.

Step 1: Write beat frequency formula

Beat frequency between $A$ and $C$:

$$ |n_1 - n_3| = 4 $$

Step 2: Substitute values

Given $n_1 = 1.03 n_2$ and $n_3 = 0.99 n_2$:

$ |1.03 n_2 - 0.99 n_2| = 4 $

$ |0.04 n_2| = 4 $

$ 0.04 n_2 = 4 $

Step 3: Find $n_2$

$ n_2 = \frac{4}{0.04} = 100 $

Step 4: Find values of $n_1$ and $n_3$

$ n_1 = 1.03 n_2 = 1.03 \times 100 = 103 $

$ n_3 = 0.99 n_2 = 0.99 \times 100 = 99 $

Step 5: Answer

The frequencies of fork B and C are respectively:

$$ \boxed{100\ \mathrm{Hz}\ \text{and}\ 99\ \mathrm{Hz}} $$

Correct option:

Option D: 100 Hz and 99 Hz

content: A pipe closed at one end vibrating in fifth overtone is in unison with open pipe vibrating in its fifth overtone. The ratio of $l_{\mathrm{c}}: l_{\mathrm{o}}$ is $\left[l_{\mathrm{c}}=\right.$ vibrating length of closed pipe, $l_0=$ vibrating length of open pipe]
explanation:
For a pipe open at both ends, the 5th overtone is the 6th harmonic. Its frequency is $\mathrm{n}=6\left(\frac{\mathrm{~V}}{2 l_{\mathrm{o}}}\right)=\frac{3 \mathrm{~V}}{l_{\mathrm{o}}}\,.$

For a pipe closed at one end, only odd harmonics appear. The 5th overtone is the 11th harmonic, so its frequency is $\mathrm{n}^{\prime}=11\left(\frac{\mathrm{~V}}{4 l_{\mathrm{C}}}\right)\,.$

Since they sound together, set these frequencies equal:

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \mathrm{n}=\mathrm{n}^{\prime} \\ \therefore & \frac{3 \mathrm{~V}}{l_0}=11\left(\frac{\mathrm{~V}}{4 l_{\mathrm{C}}}\right) \\ \therefore & \frac{l_{\mathrm{C}}}{l_0}=\frac{11}{12} \end{array} $$

content: Two uniform strings ' $A$ ' and ' $B$ ' made of steel are made to vibrate under same tension. If the first overtone of ' $A$ ' is equal to second overtone of ' B ' and radius of ' A ' is twice that of ' B '. Then the ratio of length of string ' $A$ ' to that of ' $B$ ' is
explanation:
Fundamental frequency,

$$ \mathrm{n}=\frac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{~T}}{\mathrm{~m}}}=\frac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{~T}}{\pi \mathrm{r}^2 \rho}} $$

Given: $\mathrm{n}_2=\mathrm{n}_3$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 2 \mathrm{n}_1=3 \mathrm{n}_1 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\ldots\left(\because n_2=2 n_1, n_3=3 n_1\right)\\ \therefore & 2\left(\frac{1}{2 l_2} \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{~T}}{\pi \mathrm{r}_1^2 \rho}}\right)=3\left(\frac{1}{2 l_2} \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{~T}}{\pi \mathrm{r}_2^2 \rho}}\right) \\ \therefore & \frac{l_1}{l_2}=\frac{2 \times \mathrm{r}_2}{3 \times \mathrm{r}_1} \\ \therefore & \frac{l_1}{l_2}=\frac{2 \times \mathrm{r}_2}{3 \times \mathrm{r}_2} \quad \ldots\left(\text { Given } \mathrm{r}_1=2 \mathrm{r}_2\right) \\ \therefore & \frac{l_1}{l_2}=\frac{1}{3} \end{array} $$

content: The pitch of whistle of an engine appears to drop by $30 \%$ of the original value when it passes a stationary observer. If speed of sound in air is $350 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, then the speed of engine in $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$ is
explanation:
Speed of sound in air is $350 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$.

The observer hears a 30% drop in frequency, so:

$$ n^{\prime}=n-\frac{30}{100} n=\frac{70}{100} n=0.7 n \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(given)$$

Here, $n$ is the whistle's actual frequency and $n'$ is the observed frequency.

By the Doppler effect for a source moving away:

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \frac{\mathrm{n}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{n}}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{v}+\mathrm{v}_{\text {engine }}}\right) \\ \therefore \quad & 0.7=\frac{350}{350+\mathrm{v}_{\text {engine }}} \\ \quad & 0.7 \mathrm{v}_{\text {engine }}+245=350 \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{v}_{\text {engine }}=150 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \end{array} $$

content: The equation of simple harmonic progressive wave is given by $y=A \sin (100 \pi t-4 x)$. The distance between two particles having a phase difference of $\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)^c$ is
explanation:
Equation of the given harmonic progressive wave

$$ y=A \sin (100 \pi t-4 x) $$

General equation of a harmonic wave

$$ y=A \sin (\omega t+k x) $$

From equations (i) and (ii),

$$ \omega=100 \pi, \mathrm{k}=4 $$

But, $\mathrm{k}=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \Rightarrow 4=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda}$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\therefore \quad \lambda=\frac{\pi}{2}\\ &\text { We also know, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \text { Path difference } \Delta x=\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi} \times \text { Phase difference } \Delta \phi \\ & \therefore \quad \Delta x=\frac{\pi}{2 \times 2 \pi} \times \frac{\pi}{4} \quad \ldots\left(\text { Given } \Delta \phi=\frac{\pi}{4}\right) \\ & \quad=\frac{\pi}{16} \mathrm{~m} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$
